verses.text: Joab sees that David's heart yearns for the banished Absalom, and arranges for a wise woman of Tekoa to plead a case before him. Joab knows the king's longing but engineers a reconciliation by craft rather than counsel. Schemes that bypass open dealing rarely heal what they touch.

Joab sees that David's heart yearns for the banished Absalom, and arranges for a wise woman of Tekoa to plead a case before him. Joab knows the king's longing but engineers a reconciliation by craft rather than counsel. Schemes that bypass open dealing rarely heal what they touch.

Joab sees that David's heart yearns for the banished Absalom, and arranges for a wise woman of Tekoa to plead a case before him. Joab knows the king's longing but engineers a reconciliation by craft rather than counsel. Schemes that bypass open dealing rarely heal what they touch.

The woman spins a tale: one son killed the other, and now the family demands the survivor's life, which would leave her childless. She frames a hard case of justice against mercy, drawing David to rule before he sees its aim. A parable can lead a heart where plain words cannot.

David promises to protect her son, even swearing it by the LORD. He is quick to extend mercy in a stranger's case. We can be far more lenient toward others' sins than wise about our own household, ruling for grace abroad while justice fails at home.

She turns the tale on David: why keep his own banished son away? And she drops a jewel — God 'doth devise means, that his banished be not expelled from him.' God seeks ways to bring the outcast home. His heart is bent on restoring, not abandoning, the exiled sinner.

The woman flatters David as an angel of God, discerning good and evil. Her words are wise, but woven through with manipulation. Even true things can be used to steer us; we must weigh not only what is said but why, and at whose prompting it comes.

David sees through it: 'Is not the hand of Joab with thee?' The woman admits Joab put the words in her mouth. The king discerns the schemer behind the speech. Wisdom listens, but also asks who is really speaking and what they are truly after.

David lets Absalom return to Jerusalem but forbids him to see the king's face. It is a half-reconciliation — pardon without fellowship, the form of mercy without its warmth. Such measured, guarded forgiveness often leaves the deeper wound unhealed.

Absalom is praised as the handsomest man in Israel, his heavy hair cut but once a year. Scripture lingers on his looks — the very glory that will help him steal the people's hearts, and the hair that will one day trap him. Outward splendour is a poor measure of a man.

After two years still barred from the king, Absalom forces the issue by burning Joab's field to summon him. He gets his way by destruction, impatient and ruthless. The reconciliation is shallow; the same self-willed spirit that murdered Amnon is plainly still at work.

Joab brings Absalom to the king, who kisses him — outward peace restored at last. But it is a kiss over an unhealed breach, with no real repentance on Absalom's side. Reconciliation that papers over sin without dealing with the heart only delays the reckoning.
